questions.question: Refer to the illustration, what is the total resistance of this circuit?
questions.explanation: Refer to the illustration, what is the total resistance of this circuit? The 1, 6 and 3 ohm resistors are in series: 1 + 6 + 3 = 10 ohms. This then makes two 10 ohm resistors in parallel. RT = R1 x R2 / R1 + R2 (10 x 10 = 100) / (10 + 10 = 20) = 5 ohms. This makes 1, 6, 3, and 10 ohm portion of the circuit look like 5 ohms to the rest of the circuit. This 5 ohms is in series with the 2 and 3 ohm resistors so they can be added together to equal 10 ohms; 2 + 5 + 3 = 10 ohms total
references: Low Voltage Wiring: Security Fire Alarm Systems 3rd Edition
Low Voltage Wiring: Security Fire Alarm Systems 3rd Edition : 276-
Low Voltage Wiring: Security Fire Alarm Systems 3rd Edition : 276-279
questions.mediaDescription: Circuit diagram popup dialog titled 'Diagram'. A 10 VOLTS battery source. Top branch left: 2 ohms resistor; bottom branch left: 3 ohms resistor. Middle: 10 ohms resistor (vertical). Right side: top 1 ohm resistor, bottom 3 ohms resistor, and a 6 ohms resistor (vertical on far right). The 1, 6, and 3 ohm resistors are in series (=10 ohms), which is in parallel with the 10 ohm resistor (=5 ohms), then in series with the 2 and 3 ohm resistors (2+5+3=10 ohms total).

questions.question: For general wiring, what type of cable containing one or more conductors is approved for direct burial in the earth.
questions.explanation: 310.7 Direct-Burial Conductors. Conductors used for direct-burial applications shall be of a type identified for such use. Cables rated above 2000 volts shall be shielded. 338.2 Definitions Service-Entrance Cable. A single conductor or multiconductor assembly provided with or without an overall covering, primarily used for services, and of the following types: Type SE. Service-entrance cable having a flame-retardant, moisture-resistant covering. Type USE. Service-entrance cable, identified for underground use, having a moisture-resistant covering, but not required to have a flame-retardant covering.

questions.question: Refer to the illustration, What type of chart is this?
questions.explanation: Refer to the illustration, What type of chart is this? Sinusoidal Waveform The waveform for alternating-current circuits is symmetrical, with positive above and negative below the zero reference level. For most alternating-current circuits, the waveform is called a sine wave or a sinusoidal waveform.
questions.mediaDescription: Enlarged Diagram popup ('Click to open image in new window'): oscilloscope grid graph of a sinusoidal (sine) waveform. Y-axis labeled 'VOLTS = .5/DIV' (vertical), X-axis labeled X, origin at 0. The curve shows multiple cycles; point A marked at a positive peak/crest and point B marked at a negative trough/valley. A dashed horizontal line marks the zero reference level with positive above and negative below.

questions.question: Any intentional alteration of frequency response including tone control is termed ____.
questions.explanation: d) Equalizers. The term equalization was originally applied to the process of electronically compensating for deficiencies in the ability of a component or system to accurately reproduce sounds or audio signals. Generally, the term (EQ for short) is now applied to any intentional alteration of frequency response including tone control-whether for practical or creative reasons.

questions.question: Refer to the illustration, What is the voltage Y to A?
questions.explanation: Refer to the illustration, What is the voltage Y to A? Looking at the diagram, note that it says .5 Volts/DIV. This is "volts per division". Each square, or division, is .5, therefore, count the divisions between Y and A, and multiply by .5
questions.mediaDescription: Diagram popup titled 'Diagram'. Oscilloscope-style waveform display on a grid. Y-axis labeled 'VOLTS = .5/DIV', X-axis labeled 'TIME = 10ms/DIV'. Shows a sine wave. Point 'Y' is marked on the y-axis (horizontal dashed reference line at the sine wave's mid/zero level). Point 'A' is marked at a wave peak (upper), point 'B' is marked at a wave trough (lower). The dashed reference from Y goes to peak A. Caption below: 'Click to open image in new window'. Belongs to Question ID 54582 / Question 87. Per the associated help, count divisions between Y and A and multiply by .5; A is 2 divisions above Y = 1 volt.

questions.question: Refer to the illustration, What is the time A to C?
references: Telecommunications Distribution Methods Manual: Telecommunication Distribution Methods Manual 12th ed Vol 1 Page 1-17, 18
questions.mediaDescription: Oscilloscope/waveform diagram (labeled 'Diagram (click to enlarge)'). A sine wave is plotted on a grid. Vertical axis labeled 'VOLTS' with '5V/DIV'. Horizontal axis labeled 'X' with 'TIME = 10ms/DIV'. Points marked on the waveform: A (first positive peak), Y (a horizontal reference/dashed line at mid-level), B (a trough/lower point), C (a later point on the dashed reference line to the right). The waveform spans from point A to point C across 5 horizontal divisions, giving 5 x 10ms = 50 ms.

questions.question: Refer to the illustration, What is the time between waves?
questions.explanation: Refer to the illustration, What is the time between waves? Note: 10 ms/DIV ( /DIV = "per division") each box, or division, is equal to 10 ms. Count the divisions and multiply by 10.
references: Telecommunications Distribution Methods Manual : 1-17
questions.mediaDescription: Oscilloscope-style diagram (click to enlarge) on a grid. Y-axis labeled VOLTS = 5/DIV, X-axis labeled TIME = 10ms/DIV. A sine-like waveform crosses the grid with point A marked at a peak (upper region) and point B marked at a trough (lower region). The time between the two waves/points spans 4 divisions = 40 ms.
